speaker: interviewer
text: That was in the picture, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Madam Lucia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is Wednesday the 8th of May, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor's name is Mrs. Lucia, maiden name Charhon, married, Amato. The interviewer, Rebecca Ziman, born Cone. We are interviewing in Cape Town, South Africa in English and Ladino. Tape one. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: One, two, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madam Lucia, I see you have decided to do the interview in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm actually honored to do these interviews, especially because I'm a child of a survivor myself. So it is very important to me to actually do these interviews. I'm going to be asking a few questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I hope you're not too nervous.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. I would like you to talk to me and tell me what you remember of your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come from Rhodes Island. 1944, the Germans took us, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- just try and-- before the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like you to tell me a little bit about your background and your family. [LADINO]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK. Yes, I understand. Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LADINO]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All right. I was a young girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters have you got?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were four sisters and three brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: They names there were-- [INAUDIBLE], the first it was-- we were eight, you know. One died, so we remained seven. One was Djoya.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, the eldest. And then Isaac. And then Caden. And then Lucia is me. And then my sister Felicie. And then my brother Matteo. And then my brother Maurice, who died also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your mom and your dad's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad is Benjamin, Benyamin. And my mother was Rosa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just tell me a little bit of stories of what you used to do and what your dad did in Rhodes Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father came from Turkey, you see, to Rhodes. And he was a wealthy man. And then with his brothers, he became a partner of [INAUDIBLE] stores. So I think for a few years, he lost all the money, you see. And then he had a store of dry foods to sell. And he was going on the life, you see. And we were very happy family. My mother she was very nice. She was a house--

speaker: interviewer
text: Housewife.

speaker: interviewee
text: --housewife, you see. So we were in the school. 1933, my sister married an Italian. So they didn't like, because they married in Italian fellow. So they say, you can't stay in the-- in the Jewish school. So I came out when I was about 13 from the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they took you out of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they said that you can't come to school, because your sister got married to an Italian.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the school told your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the school. Yeah, the family. So my-- my three-- the-- my two brothers and the sister, she went to the Italian school. And I came out. I didn't go to school again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to work in this factory, this cigarette factory, you see. And I was there happy. And after when my mother died in 1936-- What did she die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: She just was sick. And she had an operation, you see, or arrhythmia, you see. And when she was ready to come home, the operation-- the, what you call that thingy? Open, there-- where she had the operation?

speaker: interviewer
text: The stitches.

speaker: interviewee
text: The stitches broke out. And then they made her again the operation. And after a few months, she died, you know. So I was crying a lot, because my mother she was a very nice lady. And then after that, I still work in the factory of cigarettes, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year did you say she died?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother still was young, you know. And the last, Maurizio, was very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who took care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister Djoya took us care now. In 1938, the Jews they were running away from Budapest. They came to Rhodes, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before we go on to that, where you a happy family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I want to tell you about my brothers when they went to Israel in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Oh. OK. But I just would have liked you to tell me more about your family before the war. OK. Were you very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, no, just nice. Not very religious, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see my brother used to-- yes, my father used to go to synagogue every day, night, and all the time, you see. And we, the young kids, we didn't go very much like here and Cape Town, you see. They used to go, the old people. My mother, she used to go, you see. And Saturday, she never-- she used to smoke. She never smoked. She never did nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you respected Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we respected Shabbat, you know. And after when my mother died, the-- the house without the mother, you know how it is. It was very sad. I used to cry all the time when I used to come from work, you know? And then the life was going on. And what we had, you know, we-- when my father came from Turkey, he brought plenty money, you see, old money. So my sister all the time, she used to change the money. And we used to live with that thing. And plus, my father used to bring the-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember? Where were you at the time? Did you hear that there was trouble with the Jewish people during the time that you were in Rhodes Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938, you see, they said that we-- the Italian, they mustn't talk to the Jewish people. Because it's the racism was coming, you know, on. And so, but all the time, the Italian, they were so nice people. They like the Jewish people in Rhodes. And we used to talk. And they used to tell us there's people killing in Russia and things we didn't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you did-- you did hear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we knew a little bit. But we didn't know it was so hard, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew that they were harming the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't think that it would come to Rhodes.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Rhodes, yeah. We never thought so. Because the Germans, they were already in Rhodes. And they didn't touch no Jews at all. They were walking down, buying from Jewish people things. They never did nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were quite free.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember-- how it all started?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938, you know, the Jews, they were running away from Budapest. They came to Rhodes. So they had a boat. And then, all of a sudden, it took fire, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they stay in-- in the stadium. The Italians took them out from that boat. They put them in the stadium. And all the Jews of Rhodes, we took them food every day. So I used to go with the bicycle, you know, and take the food to them. And [? one ?] [INAUDIBLE], because we didn't know other language ourselves, only Italian, French, Greek, and Spanish, you see? They were talking German.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were talking Yiddish. We didn't understand. But they had little books, you know, and they were asking questions which to tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they were going to Israel. So after they went away, my two brothers decided to go together. Many Jews of Rhodes who had Italian passport, they went with this boat. And the other ones, they emigrated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Italian, they say, you-- you haven't got Italian passport. You must go away from Rhodes. So many people, they went to Tangier, you know. And they saved themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you know why these-- was it Yugoslavian, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Those people from Budapest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --why were they going to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were running, because-- they were running, because of the Germans. They say so. And they-- they run away. And they left all their families and the houses, you know. Many-- they were young fellows, not very old people, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So some of the people from Rhodes also went with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your brother, you said, went with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, two brothers. One was 14. Matteo was 14. And the other brother was about 25-- something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you feel about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We are crying when they left. And then we didn't know nothing, because they-- when they arrive in Israel, they couldn't go in also. Because the English people were there. They didn't let them go free. So they wait till 4 o'clock in the morning to run through the-- you know? The [SPANISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPANISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When they-- they just went in the night-- 4 o'clock in the night, they came out of the-- [NON-ENGLISH] you know? And they just went to Israel, there. And they were so happy there. And then we start getting letters, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, where were you at the time? Do you remember what happened when the Germans came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were in Trianta. It's a big village. Everybody went there, because they were throwing bombs in Rhodes, you know? So we was happy there. And we had a nice time with the Greek people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, they say-- I went to Rhodes. You know, it was not so much, only with the bicycle for one hour, half an hour, I don't remember. So they say all the Jewish men, they must go for a meeting. So they went-- Who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people, they told me that they call these men, you know, for a meeting. Only the men, they must go for a meeting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who called them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The President, you know, Mr. Franco. It was Mr. Franco, I think. He called them. And all the people went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was dead already. So my brother Maurizio went there. And all the other people, you see? After it was nighttime, about 7 o'clock in the night, they say they mustn't they don't-- they don't let them come out. Because they must call their wives and the family with all the gold and the money to go-- to go and meet them. So the poor people-- so everybody took things-- a suitcase, and they put a few things and the money and the gold what they had. They arrived there in that-- in that-- what do you call? It was the airport, you know, Italian airport. And we went there all together. And then we never came out, you see? And for three days we were there-- no food. So you also went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But that time, they left-- for three days, they left the door open, you know? People, they were going out and coming, you know? After three days, they closed the gates-- who was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somebody was crying, and they want to go. He said, no, you're not going from here, he said, the German. You're going through the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] what do you call the-- what they put fire? What do you call this-- A chimney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chimney, you know? And people, they said, no, I've got a Turkish passport. Say, show me. So they went to the house, and they found the passport. Three, four families, they came out from the-- from that camp. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And after three days, they put the alarm, you know, like bombing. Like, people came to put bombs, you know? And they took us to the-- what do you call-- to the-- what is the boat? What do you call the-- [SPANISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPANISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPANISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us there. And slowly, slowly-- and then, with the Germans and with the dogs, you know, they took us to the-- that port. And then we saw three little boats, you know, where they put all the coal inside. And we went in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And many people, they were sick. Many people, they-- they carried them with the [INAUDIBLE], you know? With the [NON-ENGLISH]. With the beds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And they put us there. It was terrible. They-- they-- we had no place even--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the-- to the docks-- to the port?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking. Walking. And I told you, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days did you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it far?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so far from [INAUDIBLE] to go to the port. It was not so far, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you walk for many hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, two, three hours, you know? Something like this. I don't remember now. In any case, we arrived there. And they start putting them inside, inside. So we arrived there. And we want water.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nothing, you know? And food and people crying, because many people, they didn't stay together. You see, we went, the three of us, together-- Matteo, Maurizio, Djoya, and myself, we three. Can I just ask you something? You said two brothers went to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were six of you left? You said you were, all together, eight. One died.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, one died. We were seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were four--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were all the other five.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, she was married to the Italian, I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She emigrate, 1942, to Rome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: With a family. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so there's left four.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to the concentration four. We came back three.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. OK, but now we're now at the port, at [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: We were three.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] You were three.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were four, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Felicie, Lucia, Djoya, and Maurizio.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went there, did you know what was happening to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. They said--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, you must go to this boat. And we didn't know where they are going to take us, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in the nighttime they-- is to go the boat, you know? Daytime, they were afraid in case they bombing. You see the-- the-- the English people, they used to come and bomb-- or Americans, I don't know. So they used to-- they were afraid to go in daytime. We used to go in nighttime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So daytime, we used to stop. And it was very hot. But the German, there were only seven in the boats. We didn't know where they were taking. Otherwise, we-- we could kill them, you know? So there was a big group of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that went into the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: 700 Or 800 in each boat-- I don't know-- you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't know where they were taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know. So, after nearly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take things with you-- belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you-- a little suitcase, what we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Suitcase. Did they allow you to take it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did they put-- where did they fit it all in the ships?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I say, it was funny. It was-- everything was up and down, you know? Even to go to the loo, there were-- terrible, nothing. It was very hard there.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no toilets, either.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were toilets. Mind, you know, so many people went there, it was full up. And the water was not flushing. And the headaches, from the dirt, you know? For nearly eight days we were there in the boats. So and then, after, we arrive in Pireo, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just tell me more about this boat? When you went into the-- the boat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still all together, the four of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were all together, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All together. And what were you thinking--

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to help even-- we used to help the old people, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you thinking, at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We used to cry. And we say, where are they taking us? We didn't know all these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try and escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My-- my friends-- you know, I had a Greek people. They say, stay here, Lucia. Lucia, stay here! And I say, I'm going with my family. I didn't want to leave them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they're trying to keep you behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your hide your identity during that time? Did you try and hide that you are Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then where was the boat going? Where did you sail to? [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: At-- at at Pireo. We were going to Pireo.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: But before, we went to all the islands, you know? And they-- they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why you went to all the islands?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because, I told you, like, they were stopping all the time, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: But do you know why they were stopping all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of the bombing, you know? Throwing bombs and things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So it took a long time to-- by ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we arrived at Pireo. And in the meantime, it was so hot there. We couldn't stand the heat. And they say, all the women, they must sit one side and all the men, the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a-- a [INAUDIBLE]. The mother was dead, you see? And the daughter was taking the father, who was very old. Maybe he was 60, 70. And he had a beard, you know? Beard.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] beard, yes? And then, when he was walking-- he couldn't walk, because he was blind. The daughter took him. And they were hitting the daughter. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were hitting?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German was hitting the daughter and the-- that man. [INAUDIBLE] ah, ah! And we were crying sitting there. Because he couldn't go alone. He was blind. And the daughter was [INAUDIBLE]. We used to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So another German came to ask me, well, why I'm crying. He say, I'm not German, I'm Austrian. He was sorry. Because-- he say, why you are here? I say, because Jewish. I told him, you see? He said, I'm Austrian, not German. He was not SS, you know? But he's to do his army, you know? And he was quite nice mensch him. And after-- With you, he was nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. From-- no, from all the-- when we were crying there, he asked us why we were crying and why-- why we are here. You see? And we told him, because we're Jewish. They took us, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't even know what he was doing, himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't know. You see? And after from there, they brought two lorries to put the people up. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: From [INAUDIBLE]. From Pireo--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --we went to Chaidari.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: By boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, by-- by-- by car, you know? They put two lorries.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in Pireo, when we arrived in Pireo--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --by boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: After, they brought two lorries--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to put the people, to take us-- I don't know-- 50, 60 miles away from Pireo. They had a camp. Because there were plenty people there still from Salonika. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived there, they say, oh, you arrive here. Now, they are going to take [INAUDIBLE] to Auschwitz. Where is Auschwitz, we ask.

speaker: interviewee
text: They say it's in German. Because already they took many people. And we were the last, they say. Because in 1944 the war is nearly finished. And so you arrive here. Now, they are going to take us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there three, four days. And we used to speak with these people in Spanish or Greek, you know? And after, they took us for Auschwitz. And they put us the train. So from-- you went by bus--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, by--

speaker: interviewer
text: By truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: By truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: By truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And many people, they were fat, you know? They couldn't go up. They used to hit the people, you know? And you used to cry. In the meantime, they hit! People died there. Many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they hit them with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they hit the people with?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had bastones, you know? [NON-ENGLISH] They had-- ooh, it was very strong when they used to hit you, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes, they hit me also. Because-- [NON-ENGLISH]. They used to say, run! They tell we could run. But the old people, they couldn't run, like my auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 24, nearly-- 23 and a half. 24, say.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 24 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I looked like 17 at the time, you know? I was thin. And we were happy. And yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when they put you in 0 trucks, how long were you traveling in the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. After, when we went to Chaidari--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed, I told you, like, two hours, three hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we stopped there for three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after, they took us again to-- to the trains where they carried the animals, you know? The truck for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel, when you saw this train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We didn't know where they're taking us, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: You just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was crying, everybody-- come here, [NON-ENGLISH]. And everybody, we went through there, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just followed the leader, there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the leaders.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were just telling you where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they put there, there is the-- there were a barrel of water, something like this. And we sit in the floor with the suitcases what we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: --with us, in the truck. And every-- the train, it was already full.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of train was is?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, like, a track for the animals-- what do you call them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle train, you know? And everybody sitting the floor, and it was dirty and everything. And then we had the journey--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, I don't know, four, five, six, or eight days? I don't remember, really. Because myself, these days, I'm getting for forget everything, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember how long you were traveling in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it two days? Was it two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe two weeks, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the train stop anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they used to stop for water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they used to come--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean drinking water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Water-- drinking water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who used to bring you the drinking water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- we. You see? They used to take, [NON-ENGLISH] Outside! We used to go down. And the waters of the toilets, you know, they used to go up, the German, and throw away the water, the dirty water on top of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, dirty water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wee, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the buckets full of--

speaker: interviewee
text: The buckets of-- yeah, they used to throw. And it was very hard in the train, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: They used to throw it on who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on our people. We used to come out from the truck--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the train. And they used to throw that water, dirty, on top of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over you. In the meantime, the young fellows, they used to bring the water--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to put in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you carry the water in? Did they give you special barrels?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They used to have a-- a barrel. And these young fellows, Jewish fellows, they used to take up on the train--

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was your drinking water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the drinking water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No food. I think-- I remember they put some watermelon. [INAUDIBLE] I don't-- a piece of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Watermelon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dried things, yeah. And then we were there in the train. All of a sudden, it start going, the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were without food for 15 days.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- we stop and the-- the every thing. Like, [INAUDIBLE], we stop. And I don't know where we stop in any case. There were Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: They used to bring plenty food, never gave us nothing. Never. You see? So the people, they were throwing things-- little apples, a piece of bread. Never mind, they gave us bread. Who was throwing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people that were there. Maybe they-- the civilians from countries which we used to pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: They used to throw it in the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with the thing. Yeah. And the bread, inside there were little knives, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Knifes?

speaker: interviewee
text: To cut the wood, to escape. But we didn't know nothing-- how can you leave?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean there were knives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Little knives they put in the bread--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to help you running away when they stop the train-- to cut the train pieces, you know? And then [INAUDIBLE] through. But we didn't leave the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't realize what it was for.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. And that's all. And then, after-- after a few-- it was a week or two weeks, I don't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people started dying in the train, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, plenty. Yeah, plenty. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- did you take them out of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the ladies, they were expecting, also-- I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take them out of the train when they were dying during the journey? You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in my-- in my train there, nobody died. Plenty, they were fainting. But in the other trains, you know, the people they had-- people that were expecting, people, old people-- I think-- I knew a man they throw him in the sea. Near [INAUDIBLE], you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Levy. And one, he died in Athens. Mr. [INAUDIBLE], he died there with the heats. But all that was, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrive in Auschwitz, it was in the morning. We were so happy to-- oh, thank god, we arrive to stretch our legs. And we saw so many people there. We didn't know what it was. And all [INAUDIBLE], what do you call these, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPANISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPANISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The fence, yeah. You see? And then, when we were there, we were happy there. And then, in the meantime, there were the Germans, the SS, with the dogs. And they should take one from here, one from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my auntie said, come with me. Help me. So my sister, she passed already with the young people. The one, she died there. She said, Lucia, come this way! Come this way! So when I went, the other German saw me arrive from the-- that line, at there. He hit me in the face, you know? And then I was happy with them, with my sister, the three of us. And my brother was separate, because-- so when we went to the camp where they put these [SPANISH]-- what you call? The-- yes, the fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fence. And we saw my brother was in Lager B. We were in Lager A, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you realize what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And then when they start crying, we cry, you know? Ay, ay! The other people say, I want my mother. I want my father. You know, I didn't have my father and mother, died in Rhodes, you see-- but the other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Salonika people, who knew, they took them two years before, you know? And they say, oh, my-- my [INAUDIBLE]. Don't cry, because your mother and father, they're already in the crematorium. And see, look there. This is smoke where is the people burn, you know?
